And essentially, they're not charging you to access Facebook. They need to find another way. And that's where monetization comes through in ads. In order to make the ads as best as they can, they use your data as a user to bring that to advertisers and hopefully allow the advertiser and the consumer to have a match between a product or a service that the user finds useful. So for example, let's say you've got medical condition and you do a search for it. Well, guess what Google know about that? Then you take it to another level where Amazon actually have far more data than people realize because not only do they run an e-commerce operation and know exactly what you're buying and when, but they also have their technology in the background, Amazon Web Services, which manages like hundreds of thousands of other websites and they're collecting data about you from all these sites. So it's not just Facebook that are responsible for data, it's all these big companies. What the GDPR is trying to do is to make sure that we are all following a similar process where privacy and data protection is a core part of your business for too long it's been an afterthought so people have started businesses, entrepreneurs whatever it is and then data and privacy is just bolted on either when there's a problem or when there's a technical person that comes in and says hey what are you doing with this you know CSV file over 100,000 customers are just sitting in this public drive so that what GDPR is trying to get people to focus on is privacy is actually part of a core part of your business and that's probably the safest way to make sure you're on the right side of the law or the regulation when it comes to GDPR. So it includes a right to access, right to be forgotten, data transparency, personal data. So personal data is anything identifiable to that individual person. The processing of the data, whether it's manual or automated, and also how the records are kept and kind of how people can get access to that as well. Then kind of difference between subject, controller and processor. A subject is the consumer or the customer, the person whose data it belongs to. The controller is the person collecting it and the processor is the person or entity that's actually doing something with the data. So if a customer gives you an email address, they're the subject, you're the controller because you're collecting the email. Then let's say you put it into a customer audience, then Facebook is your data processor. And so that's how that setup works.
